draft.introduction: Cicero’s De Amicitia (On Friendship) is a dialogue on the nature of true friendship, placed in the mouth of Laelius after the death of Scipio Africanus. It argues that friendship is founded on virtue and is among the greatest gifts of human life.

translation.text: Quintus Mucius the augur used to recount many things about his father-in-law Gaius Laelius in a pleasing manner and from memory, and did not hesitate to call him wise. I was introduced to Scaevola by my father when I assumed the toga of manhood, and from that point I never left the old man’s side, so far as I was permitted.
translation.original_text: Q. Mucius augur multa narrare de C. Laelio socero suo memoriter et iucunde solebat

translator_notes.note: Cicero’s emphasis on friendship (amicitia) as inseparable from virtue (virtus) distinguishes his account from utilitarian models. True friendship cannot exist between the wicked—a position that influenced Aelred of Rievaulx’s Christian theology of spiritual friendship.
